nativeRange.habitat: Hill streams to major rivers, irrigation canals, ponds, lakes, and ditches in shallow quiet zones with submerged vegetation or leaf litter; Western Ghats mountain drainages in Goa, Karnataka, Kerala, and Tamil Nadu

decorPreferences: dense planting (Java fern, Cryptocoryne, Vallisneria, floating plants)
leaf litter (Indian almond, oak leaves) for natural biotope tannins
open mid-water swimming lanes for schooling and male display

diet.notes: Wild diet includes diatoms, algae, organic detritus, small insects, worms, crustaceans, and zooplankton. In captivity the Melon Barb accepts high-quality dried flakes and micro-granules as a staple; supplement regularly with small live or frozen foods (bloodworm, Daphnia, Artemia) to bring out the best colour. It is a vigorous feeder and may outcompete slower or more timid tankmates at feeding time.

avoidWith: very small or slow-moving fish that may be out-competed at feeding
long-finned varieties of the same or other species (minor fin-nipping risk in small groups)

imageCandidates.notes: Photographer: Irvin Calicut. Wild specimen photographed in Thottumukkam, Kerala, India, September 2015. Shows typical banding pattern in natural habitat context.
Photographer: Nativeplants garden. Photographed June 2024 at ~12°N 75°E (Karnataka/Kerala region). 4080×3060 px.
FishBase species ID 24700. Multiple images including specimen from Karnataka, India (Regional Research Centre, CIFA, 2001, 50 mm). CC-BY-NC applies to at least one image on page.

summary: The Melon Barb is a small, peaceful schooling cyprinid endemic to India's Western Ghats. Males display three bold black vertical bars on a silvery-green body with a distinctive reddish-melon flush on the belly during courtship. It does best in soft, slightly acidic water and should be kept in groups of 8–10 or more.
careNotes:
The Melon Barb is native to southern India, where it inhabits streams, rivers, irrigation canals, and ponds across the Western Ghats drainages of Goa, Karnataka, Kerala, and Tamil Nadu. The species has a long taxonomic history: first described by Jerdon in 1849 as Cirrhinus fasciatus, it was subsequently moved into the genera Barbus and then Puntius before researchers erected the replacement genus Haludaria in 2013 — the earlier proposed name Dravidia having already been claimed by another group of animals. The current scientific name is Haludaria fasciata. The common name can cause confusion, as "Melon Barb" is occasionally applied informally to unrelated fish; it should not be confused with the Melon Discus, a colour form of Symphysodon aequifasciatus from an entirely different family.

A minimum group of 8–10 individuals is strongly recommended. Males display their most vibrant reddish-melon belly colouration and compete actively in the presence of rivals, so larger, mixed-sex schools bring out the best behaviour and colour. Solitary specimens or very small groups tend to show washed-out colour and signs of stress. A footprint of 90 cm × 30 cm (approximately 80 L) is the practical minimum for a group of this size, though a larger tank of 120 L or more is preferable to ease male competition and allow open swimming lanes.

Water should be soft to moderately hard (gH 2–10, approximately 36–179 mg/L CaCO₃) and slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.5). The recommended temperature range is 22–26 °C; because the species originates from hill streams that can run relatively cool, it may adapt well to unheated or lightly heated setups in temperate climates. The tank is best decorated with dense planting, leaf litter for natural tannins, smooth river pebbles, and driftwood to replicate the hill-stream biotope. A filtration turnover of 4–5 times the tank volume per hour is generally advised.

The Melon Barb tends to be a vigorous feeder and may outcompete slower tankmates at mealtimes; feeding in multiple spots or using targeted delivery helps ensure all fish receive food. High-quality flakes and micro-granules serve as a reliable staple, supplemented regularly with small live or frozen foods such as bloodworm, Daphnia, and Artemia to support the best colouration. The species is assessed as Least Concern on the IUCN Red List (2010), though it remains a range-restricted endemic of the Western Ghats.
breedingNotes: The Melon Barb is an egg-scattering free spawner that provides no parental care, and adults will readily consume their own eggs if not removed. Conditioning males and females separately on live and frozen foods for one to two weeks before spawning generally improves results. A dedicated breeding tank of 40–60 L, fitted with a mat of fine-leaved plants such as Java moss or hornwort — or a spawning mop — positioned over a mesh bottom helps protect the eggs from the adults before they can be separated. Slightly warmer, soft, and acidic water (pH 6.0–6.5, gH 2–4) combined with a gradual morning temperature rise to around 26 °C can trigger spawning activity. Females scatter adhesive eggs among the plants, and the adults should be removed immediately after spawning. Eggs hatch in approximately 24–48 hours, and the fry become free-swimming within 3–4 days. First foods should be infusoria or a commercial liquid fry food, progressing to micro-worms and finely crushed flake as the fry develop. Breeding is considered moderately challenging because it requires a separate tank and attentive egg management.

fish.breedingNotes: The Melon Barb is an egg-scattering free spawner with no parental care. A dedicated breeding tank with fine-leaved plants or a spawning mop over a mesh bottom is needed to protect the eggs, and adults must be removed immediately after spawning. Eggs hatch in approximately 24–48 hours; the fry are free-swimming within 3–4 days. First foods are infusoria or liquid fry food, progressing to micro-worms and finely crushed flake.
